advaita.rendering: Fix the mind (manas, the faculty of sankalpa-vikalpa) on Me alone — on Ishvara in His universal form — and lodge the discriminating intellect (buddhi) in Me as its sole object of determination. Having done so, you will dwell in Me, as Me (mad-atman), after the body falls — of this there is no doubt (na samshayah). Shankara reads 'mayi' as viishvarupe Ishvare, not a personal deity: absorption into the universal form dissolves the false distinction between knower and known.
advaita.bhashya_anchor: Shankara: 'mayi eva vishvarupe Ishvare manah sankalpavikalpat-makam adhattsva; mayi eva adhyavasayam kurvatin buddhim adhattsva niveshaya... mad-atmana mayi nivasam karishyasi eva atah sharirapata-at urdhvam. Na samshayah.'

vishishtadvaita.rendering: Because this path yields the supreme human goal (atishayita-purushartha), is easy (sulabha), and yields results quickly (achira-labhya), fix the mind on Me — the supremely attainable (parama-prapya) — and lodge the intellect in the firm conviction 'He alone is the supreme goal.' Once that conviction-preceded mental fixation is complete, you will dwell in Me immediately and without further obstacle. Ramanuja emphasises Bhagavan as the ultimate object of all striving, making the verse a direct soteriological promise.
vishishtadvaita.bhashya_anchor: Ramanuja: 'atishayita-purushartha-tvat sulabhatvat achira-labhyatvat cha mayi eva manah samadhana kuru... aham eva parama-prapyah iti adhyavasaya-purvaka-mano-nivedana-anantaram eva mayi nivasishyasi.'

dvaita.rendering: Fix mind and intellect exclusively on Me, Hari, who is eternally distinct from and superior to the jiva — and you will dwell in Me after the body falls. The word 'mayi' (in Me) preserves the eternal ontological distance: the liberated soul abides near Hari in bliss, not merged into Him. NOTE: No direct Madhva bhashya commentary is supplied for this verse in the payload; this rendering is inferred from Dvaita siddhanta principles rather than attested prose.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: ABSENT — payload reads 'Sri Madhvacharya did not comment on this sloka.' Rendering is siddhanta-based inference only.

shuddhadvait.rendering: Because Krsna possesses unparalleled shakti (asadharan-vastu-shakti), any form of connection (yena kena sambandha) — even the mere impulse of the mind — is enough to lodge everything in the manifest, luminous Purushottama. The word 'eva' (alone) expels all other dharmas as rivals: this is the seed (bija-bhuta) of the total surrender to be announced later in the text. After such inward absorption one dwells in Krsna Himself, not in any impersonal abode (na tu aksharadau dhamni).
shuddhadvait.bhashya_anchor: Vallabha: 'asadharanavastu-shaktya-ashraye yena kena sambandha mano-nivesha-matren... madeeyam eva kuru... atah urdhvam mayi eva nivasishyasi; na tv aksharadau dhamni; kintu mad-rupa iti bhavah.'

bhakti.rendering: Fix the mind — whose nature is sankalpa and vikalpa — steadily on Me, and lodge the resolute intellect (vyavasayat-mika buddhi) in Me as well. One who practises this receives the gift of jnana through My grace (mat-prasadena labdha-jnanah), and at the end of the body (deha-ante), after death, will certainly dwell in Me — there is no doubt. Sridhara appends a shruti citation: 'At the end of the body, Deva reveals the supreme Brahman' — grounding the verse in textual authority beyond the Gita itself.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Sridhara: 'mat-prasadena labdha-jnanah san atah urdhvam dehante maranantaram mayi eva nivasishyasi... tatha cha shrutih: dehante deva-s tarkam parabrahma vyachashte iti.'

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Having praised saguna upasana throughout this section, Krsna now states the supreme surplus: lodge the mind in Me, the saguna Brahman, making all mental modifications (mano-vrttih) Krsna-directed; lodge the intellect in Me, making all cognitive determinations (buddhi-vrttih) Krsna-directed — abandoning all other objects. The result: having gained jnana, you will dwell in Me, the pure Brahman (shuddhe brahmani). Madhusudana holds both registers simultaneously — Krsna as personal deity is the entry-point, shuddha Brahman is the terminal abode.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Madhusudana: 'mayi eva sagune brahmani manah sankalpavikalpatmakam adhattsva... labdha-jnanah san mad-atmana mayi eva shuddhe brahmani eva atah urdhvam etad-dehante. Na samshayah natra pratibandha-shanka kartavya iti.'

so_what_questions: The verse commands mental fixation (mano-nivesha) before intellectual conviction (buddhi-nivesha) — does the order matter, and what happens when intellect precedes the heart?

Shankara reads 'mayi' as the universal-form Ishvara; Vallabha reads it as the manifest personal Purushottama; Ramanuja reads it as the supremely attainable personal Bhagavan — are these three pointing at the same referent or at genuinely different soteriological objects?

The verse makes an unqualified promise — 'you will dwell in Me, there is no doubt' — yet Chapter 12 has already acknowledged that direct fixation is hard for embodied beings. What makes this assurance credible rather than merely motivational?
Sridhara appends a shruti citation to secure the verse's claim. What does it reveal about the relationship between smriti (Gita) and shruti (Upanishads) in bhakti-philological hermeneutics?
Vallabha's reading turns 'eva' (alone) into a seed of total dharma-surrender to come later. How does a single grammatical particle carry that weight, and what are the interpretive risks?
Madhusudana holds saguna bhakti and shuddha Brahman as start and finish of the same arc. Is this synthesis methodologically stable, or does it privilege one pole under the guise of synthesis?

everyday_applications.advaita: In a moment of decision-fatigue — when sankalpa-vikalpa (the mind's weighing and counter-weighing) consumes all energy — practise treating the universal ground of awareness itself as the settling object. Notice that the background in which all options appear is already still; rest the decision-mind there before choosing.
everyday_applications.vishishtadvaita: Before beginning a project or a difficult conversation, name your highest value explicitly to yourself: 'This person, this work, is the parama-prapya right now.' That prior conviction organises all subsequent effort as kainkarya (service) rather than self-assertion, making the outcome less anxious.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Cultivate the practice of recognising your own finitude clearly — you are not the author, Hari is. In a team or family context, this means letting a wiser authority (a mentor, a principle) carry the load of final judgement, while you contribute fully within your distinct role without collapsing into the leader.
everyday_applications.shuddhadvait: When you feel distant from what matters most, Vallabha's teaching says: any point of genuine connection is enough — a single sincere impulse toward the beautiful or the good counts. Do not wait for a perfect devotional state; begin with whatever partial contact is available now.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Build a daily practice where both the feeling-mind (sankalpa) and the reasoning-mind (buddhi) are aimed at the same object — a teacher, a text, a quality of presence. Sridhara's 'mat-prasadena' (by grace) reminds us that jnana is received, not produced; the practice creates receptivity, not the outcome.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: When meditation on a personal form feels shallow and conceptual practice feels cold, Madhusudana's synthesis offers a path: begin with the personal image, let it warm the heart, then notice that the warmth is not bounded by the image. The saguna entry-point and the nirguna abode are the same movement at different depths.

self_assessment: Six genuinely divergent renderings anchored in supplied bhashya prose; Madhva absence flagged explicitly; no collapsed or platitude-level applications; Sridhara treated from clean Sanskrit payload; all constraints met.
